sentences.text: In 1939, it was, like, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Polish and German war.
It started in '39.

During that period, in '39, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was-- like I, I-- the, the, the, the, the Polish government, what, and the city, like, the mayor from the city, they organized some-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how do you call it-- like, a militia.
It was the guard to patrol the streets, or what.
So I mean, the people, I mean, in the evenings, it was-- it wasn't any light.
It was dark, because--
--the-- yeah, curfew.
Because the planes used to fly around and used to bombard there.
And so, shall you say, I, I been in patrolling the, the streets with somebody else, not by myself.
Somebody else, too, used to-- used to go, or what.
And, like, our city, Ostrog, it was-- they had a, a natural border, like a river.
The river, its name was Vilna.
So we used to go to the river.
And we used to see-- there-- it was the river, and it was then woods.
Through the woods, we used to see the lights from the Russian Army, moving closer, and closer, and closer.
And then, when, the-- when the Germans was to take over all Poland, they work it under-- I don't recall the, the city.
Well, then Stalin made a pact with Hitler.
They dividing the Poland in two parts.
One part went to Germany.
The other part went to Russia.
So-- and I was on the side from Russia.
So since '39, I been there in Russia.
Well, in the first days, I mean, we heard a lot about it.
But we couldn't comprehend that this is real, the way people used to tell us, or what.
First of all, they took over all the stores-- the Russians-- all the stores, all the factories.
They took-- they took the-- the wealthier people, they sent them out to Siberia.
And right away, they formed lines.
Lines?
This is the first time we ever see it.
I mean, if you need a piece of bread, you got to stay in line.
It, it-- wasn't any bread in the beginning.
There wasn't any bread.
So you used to stay in line.
I mean, it's a city for so many-- 10,000 Jews only, except the gentiles, or what.
And it was-- in the beginning, it was many, many stores opened.
And so we used to get up about 4:00 in the morning.
Sometimes we used to stay there all night in line to get a bread of-- a loaf of bread.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't so, so peaceful.
I mean, the police, the militia, used to go around.
And they didn't let us form lines on the streets.
So we used to run around, around the streets, all over.
Shall we say, before I was in the line that turned on the front, then I came back.
I was only at the end of the line.
And when it used to come, several times, I get it.
I used to come to my turn.
They said, there is no bread.
I used to go home without bread, or what.
Well, at that time, I mean, no.
We-- they-- I, I didn't.
I mean, the-- like we used to work before.
They just didn't let us.
They have formed, like, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] shops, their own.
I mean, they took it over, like, it wasn't private enterprises.
And everything belongs then start to belong to the--
--to the government.
And they-- and they formed, like, shops, lots of them.
And we start to work for them.
[SIGHS]
How, how to put it?
We used to work the whole week.
And when it comes, and Friday is one-- it was one-week pay.
We got paid after Friday, or whatever, or what.
But when it used to come to Friday, there is no money.
There was no money.
I mean, the bank hasn't got any money.
So we didn't get paid.
So what-- it was-- I mean, what people used to do-- I mean, you got to make a living.
You got to make a living.
I mean, we used to stay in the lines.
They just to give some goods, shall we say, material, or what.
I got into the store.
And I need, shall we say, fur-coat material.
When I got there-- they had it before.
When I got there, they haven't got it already.
It's gone.
What shall I do?
I don't know.
I don't need it.
What shall I do?
I, I-- I've, I've been standing there almost all night.
Behind, behind me have been some people.
They says, if you don't need it, take it anyways.
If you don't need it, somebody else need it.
You're going to sell it.
So that's what we did.
So I took it.
And I sold it to somebody else.
So I make a few bucks.
That's what-- that's how it was in the beginning.
That's the way I lived over the, the period, or what.
And that was, I mean, till '41, we lived this way.
I mean, like, cultural-- they opened clubs.
They used to-- it wasn't what to eat.
But this part, like, cultural, they opened clubs.
They, they got some music.
People-- like, I mean, I was there, a young guy.
What, what do I need?
If I had a piece of bread, I'm OK.
In the evening, I used to go 8 o'clock.
It was music.
It was songs.
And so we used to be there most of the time.
And again, I mean, you, you don't know the language.
You got to learn the language, the Russian language.
So they opened some schools, night schools.
So I went to night school.
And I learned the, the language, or what.
And this is the way we lived till '41, till the war started.
Well, there wasn't any Jewish schools at all.
There wasn't any cheder, any Jewish school.
There wasn't anything then.
Right away, they closed it, or what.
But synagogues it was.
They still, I mean, they didn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] turn down the synagogues.
They remained.
But people didn't go.
Because they were afraid.
I mean, between, between us, you, you, you could be my best friend.
But if you-- during the Polish times, you belonged to-- it was a communist organization at that time.
It was illegal.
But it was.
They used to work under the Underground, or what.
So they knew everyone.
They knew from the city.
Because, I mean, they've, they've been in the same work with I, as tailors or whatever.
So they knew each other.
And so they knew if you're going to go to the synagogue or to something else, they're, they're going to report to the militia.
And then they'll come and get you.
And they'll put you in jail.
There was no courts.
I mean, they used to have courts.
But the courts was like dictatorship.
I mean, it was a lawyer.
But it didn't do anything for you.
I mean, you're guilty, what.
That was at the beginning, as I remember, my recollection.
Well, in 1941, so-- Hitler [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started the war in, in England.
And I mean he started to, to take over-- London, and then he took-- he took over-- he-- Poland, the Czechie, all this-- the little countries.
And then they-- he came over to, to our city, I mean.
Then when it started-- in 1941, the war with Germany.
So right in the third or fourth day, were the-- the Germans was just close to our city.
So then the Russian Army, what, went-- the soldiers went from house to house, where it was young kids.
They told them to go out, out of the city, in another city, over the border.
And within two days, we're going to return back.
So we went.
Like, me, for instance, I had brothers and sisters and my, my mother.
And they all have been married with kids.
And I was single.
I was a young boy.
So I didn't want to-- I didn't want to go by myself.
So we'll-- everything-- all of the brothers and the sisters, I mean, they came over to our house.
They brought all the suitcases, I mean, knapsacks what, what they can-- could take with them.
And they told me, you go.
And we'll come after you later.
And so we went-- I went.
And, and it was, like I told you, I find my, my nephew.
And in Slavuta, we stayed overnight.
Nobody is there.
I've been looking for them.
Nobody came back.
So it's me, another three guys, friends of mine, they been in the same-- in the same shuls as I am.
So they said, you know what?
Let's go back to Ostrog, see what's happened.
So we went a half a day.
Like I told you, I showed you the book, [NON-ENGLISH], or what.
And there it was, a, a, a Russian post there.
And he didn't let us in, in there-- in, in the city because it was-- the city was already occupied.
Over-- it was-- I mean, killed people because from the bombardment that was there.
And nobody-- they didn't let us-- So this is still 1941?
This was still 1941, beginning of 1941, or what.
So we, we returned back to Slavuta there.
Coming there, the people who lived there, they start to evacuate.
So we went again.
Like I told you before, we went to Shepetivka.
And from there, we start to run.
So we went to Kiev.
And in Kiev-- All right, who's 'we' at this time?
Still your--
So--
Yeah.
Me and my nephew and my wife.
Now-- before, she was a girlfriend.
We used to go together out.
And her sisters and brothers-- no brothers.
But her sisters with their husbands, two sisters and two husbands.
Because they both together.
And they got in the same train as we were.
So from then, we went to Kiev all together.
And in Kiev, the Russian Militsiya-- so we went, like, in the city.
So we went-- we went there-- it was a lot of them, people from everywhere.
It's not only from Ostrog because it was immigrants, I mean, plenty from all over Poland coming there.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there the Militsiya went out.
And they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] circled around a section.
And they took us under-- in a park.
And there it was, like, they need some soldiers.
I mean, like Germany been running like, like crazy.
And everything what it was, the Russian left.
But before that, I mean, being in, in, in Russia at the beginning, there was, like, the Ukraine, it was a national party, Ukraine-- Ukrainish party, Underground, too.
And there was some spies when the war started.
And they had-- they got communication with the, the German line, the German front.
It wasn't just, like, ordinary people.
That was generals.
Generals from the army-- it was there.
And they had-- they had contact with each other.
And that's why Germany took over right away.
I mean, all the generals with the armies stayed there.
They didn't-- they didn't fight, or what.
So they-- that's why they was so running.
Because it was a, a fallout, or what.
So when we went to Kiev-- now I start it again.
Mm-hmm.
When we went to Kiev, they put us in a-- in the park.
And there was a-- how you call it?
I mean, like doctors in the-- and they make checkups.
They got to have soldiers from the army.
Because the majority from Ukraine, soldiers of Ukraine, they remained at the places there.
So they need in, in-- a German.
And he, he, he was right beside us, or what.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then they took us in there and-- for checkups.
And there are people-- it was single people.
I mean, like, I was single.
And they, they remained there at the place.
And they gave him-- I mean, they said, you are mobilized in the army.
And they didn't let them go already.
And now, my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the sister-- the sisters of my wife, had their husbands there, too.
But one, the older sister's husband, they had a small baby, or what.
And they remained him, too.
They said they won't let him go.
But his wife came into the office and start to cry with the baby.
And they let him out.
They let him out to go with-- And then the second sister, the same thing as the husband.
They got-- they, they been married during the war, or what.
And she was pregnant.
So she run in there and start to cry.
I mean, 'I'm pregnant and by myself.' So they let him out.
Now it's my turn.
I got in there.
I have nobody, nobody.
I don't have kids, a brother, nobody.
So I went in there.
And they, they admitted me.
I mean, that's it.
Nobody to stand up for me.
And I don't know what's happened.
And all of a sudden, my nephew showed up there, in the office.
And he started to cry.
Well, [CRYING] [MOAN] [CRYING] I'm sorry.
[CRYING] Then he start to cry.
And I don't know what's happening.
They let me out.
I survived without.
So since then, we've been together.
So we start to walk further.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We walked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came to a-- to a place.
I mean, they checked the passport.
We haven't got passports.
Because I had my-- I, I, I, I hadn't got a passport.
The brothers here, here-- brother-in-laws, one they took, the Polish harbor in '39.
So you run away from there?
So yes, he got a passport.
And the other one hasn't got a passport neither.
So they, they kept us on the-- on the checkpoint.
So I don't know.
I don't remember what's happened.
And they let us go.
Then I met-- during the period when we we've been walking, I met my niece from Ostrog and my nephew-- I showed you in the picture there-- and their mother, my sister-- my sister-in-law. or what.
So since then, we went all together, or what.
And we went to a-- to a point.
I don't know where it was.
And they took us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with a train to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dnepropetrovsk.
Then the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you call this-- call kolkhoz.
Like a kibbutz-- like, I mean, in Israel, it's kibbutzim.
In there, it was called kolkhoz.
I mean, all the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what had been working on the fields, on the ground, that was kolkhoz.
And they had, like, cattle and, and wheat, and all, all kinds of things there.
And they need some workers because the most from the village went into the army.
So I-- we went there.
And we start to work on the-- in the kolkhoz, or what.
We worked there.
So they, they fed us.
And that's all what it was.
So we been staying there, shall we say, for a month.
And we got to run.
We ran further.
We run to Rostock.
The Germans were still advancing?
They are still advancing.
They are still advancing.
We went into Rostock, Rostock, Rostock, yeah.
What-- there, they took us in-- into a, a farm, that's it's called, like a farm, or what.
And we started to work there.
We worked there for a couple of-- a couple of months.
And then again, we got to run.
It's approximately-- it's from the beginning of '41 in, in June.
It was almost about August-- June, July, August, I mean, this period of time.
So we run farther.
So we went, like-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] from Ostrog, we went already-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was the last destination.
We went to Saratov.
They took us.
And they took us to Saratov.
And there it was a German colony, in Saratov.
It was a German colony.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the war, the Russians threw out the Germans.
They took them I don't know where, to Siberia, or whatever.
Because some, they expected, were spies.
So the whole village left.
And it was empty houses.
And this was already in September, October-- it was already the fall or the winter.
It got into winter, like, the end of November, December, or what.
And we got in there.
It was cold already.
We haven't got any clothes on us.
I had a shirt and a, a pair shoes, I mean, like summer shoes.
They were already torn.
And we got to take out the grain from the fields because from the summer it's left.
They didn't take it off.
And everything was under the snow.
So it wasn't any wheat, any corn.
It wasn't anything to eat.
So when we got there, we got to start to clear it up.
So, so we went there.
It was, like, December, I think.
It was there.
And it was, like, 10 kilometers from where we lived there.
And, I mean, it's-- at 8 o'clock, it's still dark at that time.
So we used to get up at 7 o'clock.
They used to-- they used to take us off horses and wagons.
I mean, it's late then, because if wagons didn't go, it was already everything frozen in snow.
Till we got there, it was about 12 o'clock in the afternoon.
So we start to work.
What, we worked maybe two hours, or three.
We got to get back because of the-- it's already dark.
But how we-- could we, we work?
The first priority, it was-- the first priority, it was to stay alive, or what.
So what I used to do, we used to take some grain in the pockets.
And we used to come home.
There was, like, a room-- a little room.
And it was, like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- how to explain it?
It was like a-- it's not a stove.
It was built like this in the middle of the house.
And inside was, like a wok, but a big Chinese wok what you cook in it.
But this was in a big [NON-ENGLISH].
I used to put in water-- cold water there.
And it was a little kitchen.
It was a oven.
And we used to heat the oven.
And it used to warm up the water.
And we used to sit there with the feet in the kettle and warm up.
So this is if we had something to heat up the, the oven.
But if you haven't got anything-- we used to get up at night.
And we used to-- and, and and we used to get into a house and break everything.
It wasn't anything much there.
But if it's where-- if it's, what, wood, we used to break it.
And we used to take it home.
But the, the, the Militsiya looked after us, too.
If you, you got caught, you got jail, or what.
